commentBody:
Yet again — two days in a row now — the Wordplay link in the usual place on the Games page is broken.

(Cute puzzle, by the way.)
What a great puzzle. The NW and NE seemed easy to solve, but I can admire the skill in creating the PARALLELUNIVERSE.
This page's URL should be: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/06/30/crosswords/daily-puzzle-2026-07-01.html (It's missing the "daily-puzzle-" before the 2nd 2026.)
Pretty late in the day for my daily solve. I was totally lost thinking this was a Tuesday, when it was very early posted ballsy Wednesday already pushing Thursday.
Very clever, but I don’t think the two clues for ELAN represent different meanings.

This puzzle was neither an AGATE or a PEARL, but was nevertheless a gem.  Who doesn’t love a good semordnilap? That, plus two grid spanning revealers made for a Wednesday that diverted my attention from the Mexico - Ecuador World Cup game. Well done.

I had not noticed until I read the column that the NE and NW corners are isolated from the rest of the puzzle. 

Rules are made to be broken, I suppose, and this was plenty enjoyable.
The Games app has been glitching out, kicking me out of puzzles since NYT pushed the update this week. I don’t think I’m alone in this. Not sure why it is happening. But it needs to be fixed ASAP.
Brilliant!!!
What a delightful puzzle! Loved it! Super cute and fun.
Very clever. Enjoyed it. Good job.

Jaw dropped.  Not only the mirror corners, but WORMHOLE, PARALLEL UNIVERSE & ALTERNATE REALITY. And lots else to like. 

Good to see Jack Sprat, who could eat no fat, over what his wife could eat none of: LIEN. Also liked THAT SO over WHITE LIE: that's exactly how I respond to white lies (actually, it's what I respond to just about everything these days). 

I often feel like I'm living in an alternate reality anyway. In this regard, I recommend two fun books, Fredric Brown's "What Mad Universe" (a comedic pulpy sci-fi novel probably 75 years old by now, but still charming and full of wicked conundrums), and a recent novel by Lawrence Block that riffs on that book, "The Burglar Who Met Fredric Brown." Both really fun reads in the parallel universe genre. 

Alternate reality, BORED, TESLA, CON ARTIST all served to remind me of the alternate reality I know so well! Anyway, this one's a masterpiece and I bet will be shortlisted for many a puzzler's POY choices. Certainly it's on mine.

Today’s theme is the latest answer to that eternal question:  

    What on earth will these constructors think of next?

Very fun and clever. I got the upper left section pretty quickly—then got stuck in the upper middle by the diabolically clever bridge clue—EAST? WEST? Neither!—so ended up heading to the bottom where I found the theme. Then noticed the closed off upper corners, and immediately reflected on the trick there—then the wormhole helped me finish it off.

So, this was chock full of great theme material which contributed to the solve and my enjoyment, but didn’t give it away too fast and more twisty than the usually Wednesday (could’ve been a Thursday especially compared to what we’ve seen lately?) Maybe an early week POY contender just for audacity, originality, and some clever clues? SUSHIRICE? And WHITELIE which is almost a secondary theme clue (just a little bit off, like mirror universes?)

I’ve been kicking around a mirror universe themed puzzle, never could get a handle on it, thanks Jeffrey for showing me how it’s done and saving me the headache of trying to figure out something better.

Oh and thanks for the new clue for ORE and reminding me of my  dad, SOL.
Fun puzzle! Interesting to see 2 different takes on wormhole between this & Dylan Schiff’s puzzle from last year!

Agree with what everyone has posted so far today—great puzzle! I’ll also note that I LOVE the cat photo that accompanies the column. Was off from work today, and I had initially entered “couch” for 1A. So it is doubly apt for my solve experience in addition to being just plain adorable.

Thoroughly enjoyed this. Creative in a way that didn’t seem forced or overly complicated, which is always better. The cluing was great, loved the theme and the interplay between the top two corners. Very fun Wednesday. 

In other news, my app still will not connect to the wordplay column, yes I updated it twice, today and yesterday, I still get “page not found.” The app is broken in terms of linking to this column. Hope this can be fixed soon.

This puzzle was just like an
ALTERNATE REALITY in a 
PARALLEL UNIVERSE.

Beyond the ordinary,
i.e. phenomenal.

Thank you, Mr. Martinovic! 

(I think I'll go re-watch "Sliding Doors".)

Android app link not working but my brain is. Nice little week streak on the go so clearly I'm in a parallel universe or some alternate reality where I can actually solve puzzles! Thoroughly enjoyable!
This puzzle made me tap into my sci-fi knowledge.  What’s your favorite alternate universe? Mine was Fringe.  Although that finale was a bit hard to take.

I didn’t fully appreciate the brilliance of the construction until I studied the completed grid. The semordnilaps and alternate meanings are impressive enough, but then I saw how those separate corners are connected by a WORMHOLE. And the WORMHOLE serendipitously leads to NEIL deGrasse Tyson. Bravo!

Jeffrey, your puzzle wormed its way into my heart. One of my FAVORITES, for sure.

Ok, Jeffry, pretend a middle aged man, prematurely gray, is looking over his glasses at you.

This handsome man is a professor.  He is patient and kind in an avuncular way, but not creepy at all.  He tells you that he cannot, in good conscience, endorse constructing crossword puzzles in class.

But, then, he says as a sound track of sentimental music plays, that he'll make in exception, just this once, for having created a very enjoyable puzzle.

But only if you get the notes from the class, do the homework, and promise from now on to pay close attention to the profound wisdom this overworked, unpaid person is trying to bestow upon you.

In case you're wondering, no, I did not get 73A without crosses.  I briefly looked at it, realized that it was benzene based because it is clearly highly unsaturated.

I should have seen the three nitrogens and the six oxygens a dead give away for a trinitro compound.   But after what I guess to be about a second, I went on.  I rarely spend more than a second with any clue until late in the solve.

I say "a" TNT because there are six total possible compounds that can be described as "trinitrotoluene".   (There also could be others that are not toluenes.)

They are all really explosive.  In fact the most common isomer, the 2,4,6 isomer, is the most stable, and hence most "easily" handled.

By the way, trinitrotoluene, trinitroglycerin...anytime someone offers you something that starts with "trinitro--", be very wary.

Um, so the tricks really begin and end only in the upper half of the puzzle?  I came to the column expecting that I'd missed something.  Guess not.

I was really charmed there for a while (despite being slapped twice in the face with America's most alienating car company).  Overall a nice challenge for a Wednesday and definitely out of the ordinary.  Just kind of a letdown.

I loved the theme today, with the mirrored regions and the WORMHOLE.  That was clever.  Then PARALLEL UNIVERSE and ALTERNATE REALTITY as spanners.

Some of the filler I thought was weak.  Not a lot of AHAs and a lot of proper names and trivia.

I love the puzzles that seem daunting in the first 5 min and then crumble like fresh buttery scones. Fun to get annoyed with how TESLA was clued on the left and then a feeling of satisfaction with how TESLA was clued on the right! That man was a genius. TESLA, I mean. His birthday is in 10 days!

Nice mirror trick at the top. I only wish it had been duplicated at the  bottom, but I’ll take what I can get. Nice job!

My only issue with this puzzle is how hard I had to work to find the Wordplay column dedicated to it. Come on, people!

And who decided that Wordle is ranked higher than the daily crossword? Doesn’t really bother me, but it seems like a bit of a demotion for the revered OG game.

I half expected Spock with a goatee to appear as an animation when I finished.

(Look it up, zoomers.)

Doubling (quadrupling?) down on the trick in the bottom half would have been an unREALIstic expectation on my part, I suppose...

Very nicely done, sir.
What a fun, clever puzzle! Absolutely loved it <3

I grinned at least five times during this solve. Superb.

Now everyone on the train thinks I'm weird. Also superb.
Fun solve with some interesting fill.  Day two of bad Wordplay links, day two or random exits from the iOS app.  Bad look for the NYT…

Loved it! For no discernible reason, I started with 2A and therefore, ended with the NE and NW corners blank. It was then that I noticed they were not connected to the rest of the grid and I thought: "Ah, two Midis!"(and panicked just a touch)...

Began by solving the NE "Midi" and since I'd already discovered the two awesome theme revealers at the bottom I had that superb AHA moment when I broached the NW... (Love that moment!!)

I took a flyer and filled in the NW downs in reverse order without reading the clues, and VOILA, happy music.

Loved, loved, loved the puzzle and enjoyed going back to see the across answers in the NE.

Brilliant, definitely on my list for POY! 

(And love the constructor notes... I couldn't fathom constructing the NE and NW corners without at least a little computer-assisted iteration!)

I was proud of myself to get PARALLEL UNIVERSE with no crosses whatsoever. However, it was only at this point that I stepped back and noticed something odd: both top corners were completely closed off, usually a no no for a crossword. Fortunately, both LIEN and NEIL were filled out, so the mirror symmetry immediately jumped out, and I immediately got the gimmick. 

At this point, I did something that I never do: I slowed down and jumped back and forth between the mirror entries. Yes, the answers were the same for the downs, but it was still fun to see the different cluing for each. Normally, I try to speed solve, but slowing down allowed me to savour the puzzle for just a bit longer.

I'm sure there is some kind of moral or lesson to this. In any case, hats off to the constructor. One of the most enjoyable solves in weeks.

A propos GETS LOOSE; some may recall that when we first rescued our little pom Foxy at 8 months old, she was only 2.5 lbs. This is a problem because, although we have a fully fenced backyard (unlike some of our neighbours) the back is wrought iron rather than wood, and backs onto a bike path. Because she was teeny enough to squeeze through the bars, she got loose often, and the neighbours kids became quite skilled at helping us retrieve her. Obviously, after the first couple of escapes, we put a wire mesh all the way around, but the little imp still found ways to escape. Fortunately, never for too long (we were warned to always supervise her as she is easy pray for coyotes, hawks and owls). For those who missed it, Foxy just before we got her: https://imgur.com/a/QVNCqs8

Yay me!!! I can’t believe i managed this excellent crossword using only the Across clues!  Did it in 35:36. I never looked at the down clues. What a blast! Set myself a challenge and…. Wow!

The bottom half was the key to unlocking the top — once i had my ALTERNATEREALITY and my PARALLELUNIVERSE the right way round. I started off upside down but it was clear from the crosses which had to be which. 

I’ve never heard of chocolate called SKOR but i guessed it was different from the yoghurt called Skyr so it was probably a WORMHOLE and not a wyrmhole, and this gave me a toehold in the top half. 

But Jack had me really really stuck. Jack Black? Jack-anape? Jack-anory? The mirror solved it!  I was about to give up when i noticed that ENOLA and ALONE were backwards, so it wasn’t “tents“ because Jack couldn’t be”stnet”  and of course it was TARPS!  And it’s NEIL not Neal and a potato is not a baker or a frier or a dicer but a TUBER! 

I am BUZZING. Fantastic puzzle.

Others have said: the link to this page is broken from the app, the app is glitchy and now crashes regularly. Count me in on both of those. But what nobody else has said: I don’t want Wordle at the top of the app. Maybe some do, whatever. But why not let users of the app reorder the games in a way they prefer? Once you fix the other stuff, that is.

So,I admit that I missed the full cleverness of this puzzle until reading Sam's Wordplay column. Maybe I just needed my morning coffee!. However, in my defense, the down clueing on both upper corners were so distinctly different I missed the connection. Well done! And I did get a crossword thrill upon seeing the mirrored words of the blocked off corners. . Great Wednesday puzzle!

Semordnilap - I didn't know this term at all. I looked up some examples, which was interesting eg Erised - Desire in Harry Potter, (but Im not a Harry Potter reader so wouldn't have known). 

Also ENOLA. I thought it was odd, calling a character after the plane that dropped the bomb, but it seems the name has a longer history. I thought clues were mainly nice and interesting. 

The theme seemed complex to me, I read about it after - quite beyond me! I don't know what Mo. or WKS refers to?
This just confirms it for me, we are definitely living in a PARALLELUNIVERSE....  Joking aside, I very much enjoyed the theme of this one!

Solved it about 15 minutes, but refrained from filling the last box until I felt I fully understood the theme.

That the NW corner was a mirror image of the NE had been obvious nearly from the start---once TESLA appeared for the second time. And, of course, WORMHOLE in circles was obvious too.

Both revealers were simple as well, and then ... spent about seven minutes more searching for the rest of the theme. Will have to resort to the column and comments to discover what else I missed, because thematically this one feels incomplete to me at the moment.

So 15 minutes to "solve" the puzzle and 7 more minutes spent trying to fully understand the theme. Since it's my puzzle, I'll subtract and call it an 8 minute solve. Almost a Wednesday best! And my streak now stands at 260701.

Loved it.

Reminds me of my favorite Tuesday ever.

I must love a little
rule breaking…

Happy Wednesday all!

I had a different experience from those who have commented below in that I tried the first five rows of across clues, then switched to the overlapping downs and therefore rapidly progressed to two completed corners with TREAT and wEst in between them. Then I had to work on clues lower down which took rather longer. Realising that the circled letters would be WORMHOLE helped me fix a couple of errors.

Why must we assume that an eel is always "lurking"?? Maybe it's just hanging out in a reef because it likes it there. Or because that's where the food is. Or because that's where his family and friends life. Or because he's lost. Or because it's come there seeking gainful employment. Or maybe it's just curious. It could be a million things, but no, we must always always assume it's "lurking." That's just profiling!!!

I loved this puzzle! Managing to squeeze both PARALLELUNIVERSE and ALTERNATEREALITY in there is super impressive, and the fill was great. The cluing in the top portion was distinct enough that I didn’t notice the mirroring until I got all the way through the acrosses to the other TESLA.

With how much money they’re taking in from Games subscriptions, would it be too much to ask for a properly functioning Games app? The change to the commenting was bad enough, and now the Wordplay article is just MIA?

Amazing puzzle, though!

Worked it out quite quickly as ENOLA ALONE was a giveaway. 

It's the title of track 4 on Everything Must Go by Manic Street Preachers, one of the best British rock albums of the 90s.
I actually swore in admiration when I realized what was going on here.  Nicely done, Mr. Martinovic!

I'm a crossword enthusiast; I'm not a X-word purist.  I don't care if you break the "rules".  They're just conventions; they're not laws of nature.
All I ask is that when you break the rules, we can say that it was worth it.

Was it worth it today?
I'd give it a thumbs up...albeit not as enthusiastically as I might have wished.
The PARALLEL Minis in the NW and NE are pretty darn amazing.  And it doesn't bother me (much) that the constructor needed computer-assistance to make them.

But...
I might have wished that, upon completing the solve, the WORMHOLE caused the UNIVERSES to "open up" in some mind-blowing way.

A solver can dream, can't they?

Thanks, Jeffrey Martinovic, for an amusing puzzle!

Today's Puzzle Playlist brought to you by OMD: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rx1D6VlVpns&list=RDrx1D6VlVpns&start_radio=1 and--for you, Sam--by TMBG: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0lUL1hkfIIc&list=RD0lUL1hkfIIc&start_radio=1 From the other side of the aisle, Haydn: https://youtu.be/gum0PNqgM_s?t=910&si=O6cCXuxpqQyUN9CQ and Hindemith: https://youtu.be/v-dbA56Dl4s?t=0&si=vyPCnr3dm60D-rgv https://youtu.be/v-dbA56Dl4s?t=2996&si=FJIwD9LakwzobPHg (watch both) Happy Canada Day, to all my friends south of the border!
I've done a few other puzzles themed around wormholes but this one still felt fresh! Got the Gold Star in 7:07
Truly brilliant!

Whew. And Wow. Typical Wednesday workout for me, and... must confess that I wasn't catching on to the trick even after I finished. Had to come here to find out. Pretty amazing to be able to construct those opposite corners. And of course, a puzzle find. A Sunday from August 7, 2005 by Lee Glickstein and Nancy Salomon with the title "You are Here." Some theme answers: URBANDEODORANT NEUROCONSERVATIVE THECURRYINGGAME BUREAUBRIDGES HOURLYMATRIMONY TOWNWITHOUPURITY ALTEREDSTATURE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/7/2005&h=59a ....

Incidentally, our tech team is aware of issues some solvers are having with the app. It should be resolved if you update to the latest version, but let us know if not (you can reply here, or email nytgames@nytimes.com).

Interesting experience.  (Haven't read Sam's column, or the comments, yet.)  Had a little trouble getting started at the top of the puzzle, so I worked from the bottom.  Everything filled, except for the northeast and northwest corners.   Only then did I notice there were no connecting words to the rest of the puzzle.  What the heck, I asked myself.  Then I started answering the clues, and I soon realized what was going on.  I LOVED THIS PUZZLE!  Now I'll read what Sam and the rest of you have to say.
Quite difficult for a Wednesday in my opinion, but what an excellent puzzle! Enjoyed it :)
Got the gimmick early and thought this would be a gimme but no, had to work for it. Pretty dang clever.

Theory: living among us are humans that came from another plane of existence, where a single word spelled the same frontwards and backwards is called a ‘palindromeS’. It’s the only explanation I have come up with for why we have something called a ‘semordnilap’.

(Today I came here before completing the puzzle.  In fact, I’ve barely started - only done 32A, which I guessed with no crosses: SOFAS - oh good!)

Happy July First, everyone!  I think....  
Is this Thursday?  Did I miss a day?...
I'm not sure I'Ve eVer seen so many Vees all oVer a puzzle... though they seem to haVe sifted down toward the bottom.  
Quite a Variety...but apparently not part of the puzzle-trick, so I'm shifting into reVerse...like the upper corners.

Interesting and clever.  Shared the puzz with DHubby; we agree that Jeffrey Martinovic needs to get more sleep.
Jeffrey Martinovic can beam into our crossword universe anytime! My sci-fi loving husband thought this was the best puzzle EVER!
Such a fun puzzle!! So well done, thank you Mr. Martinovic!

I had no trouble completing the puzzle. Breezed through the two upper corners, then the rest of it. But my acquaintance with sci-fi ended after I read Stranger in a Strange Land and one other by Ursula LeGuin when they were new books. So I don't understand the meaning of  PARALLEL UNIVERSE & ALTERNATE REALITY as they relate to the puzzle, though I did see that they were semordnilaps. And WORMHOLE was meaningless to me as well as a connection to them. Maybe I'm just dense.
Wow, what a treat!  Thanks, Jeffrey.

This was such a fun one to puzzle over and eventually grok! (That usage is not just allowed but celebrated in my alternate reality.)

But what nefarious actors gave us a semordnilap puzzle when Lewis wasn't around. Have you no sense of decency?!

This was more difficult for me as a Wednesday crossword.
Difficult that is until I accepted the fact that the same answer (i.e. Tesla) had to be used twice.
Very creative. The WORMHOLE thing was icing on the cake.
The “aha moment” on this one made me smile.  Very clever puzzle!!

I liked this puzzle! Very clever use of the theme in the puzzle.

Rating:
9/10

Lewis would love this one. 
In addition to the semordnilaps in the upper corners, I noticed ROOM/Moor, NOH/Hon and LATE/Etal elsewhere and some short palindromes at the bottom, SIS and TNT. There may be more, buts that's all my poor brain could contain moving between the puzzle and here.

And thank you Sam for the cat pic.

What a fun grid. Questioned my sanity/ability to complete a crossword when the only possible answer to two questions was TESLA. Only then did I notice the circles. Once I got RM in there the fog cleared. Sneaky and clever. 

I see we’re being told the powers that be are aware of some issues. Yes. Issues. Stop messing with the main screen and for goodness sake sort out the comments page. This little square PIP nonsense is ruining my eyesight. Thank you.

I’m no techie, but I can’t find a way to update the Games app on my iPad or iPhone. It says it’s updating in background, but it ain’t. Crossword quits 5-10 x per game, and the others glitch out less frequently. 

They improved it worse.
Can’t find a way to update. Says it’s updating in the background, but it isn’t. Frustrated, please advise.
Fun puzzle!

This (Mon~Wed) has been the best 3-day start to a week in quite some time/that I can remember. For me, each puzzle was a touch more difficult than typical for that day, a welcome change. Themes were unique/cleverer than usual; very refreshing. Sunday was enjoyable too, but I think this M-W really stands out.
To quote Thor (2011): "ANOTHER!"

Am I only one that actually gets startled when the app crashes. I’m grinding trying to solve. And it actually ‘scares’ me when it crashes.  
But hey. Bright side. I’ll take a crash over a freeze anyway.  Freeze would take me over the edge. 

The only thing that really jumped out at me was 
“QB Manning” for ELI after last weeks? Hebrew kurfuffle. 

And. Though I could look it up, I wonder the last time ENOLA had a WWII clue. 

And for that dude that was apoplectic about Jewish clues. I just looked it up. LEVI P Morton was not Jewish. Though, I was thinking of you when I filled it in. 

Your goy boi. Weak sauce.

Enjoyed this one! Weirdly, I was thinking about the concept of alternative universes during my walk this morning and wondering what mine would look like, and then came home and did this crossword! Strange, or meant to be?!
Wow. Just Wow. Bravo.
I stumbled through the proverbial looking glass when I realized I had book-ending TESLAs, and both seemed correct. OHO!

I'm pretty dense some days (well, most days). I didn't get the significance of the wormhole and the mirroring of the NE/NW corners until I came here for the comments. I gotta say I'm truly impressed. Outstanding job Jeffrey!
Fix your app. It crashes way too much.
Great fun! Just finished a binge of For All Mankind, and working on the companion series Star City. A worthy aside. Thanks!
The app reports that I completed a Wednesday puzzle in 50 seconds. As if... Maybe in a different dimension.
Amazing payoff. One of my favorite crosswords, ever.
Amazing payoff. One of my favorite crosswords, ever.

One more puzzle find. A Sunday from February 20, 2005 by Elizabeth C. Gorski with the title "The Art of the Deal." Three sets of 5 square rebus answers near the center of the puzzle: 55A - (TEN)(JACK)(QUEEN)(KING)(ACE) 74A - (NINE)(TEN)(JACK)(QUEEN)(KING) and... 64A - (HEART)(HEART)(HEART)(HEART)(HEART) And then- two other answers, referencing those answers: STRAIGHTFLUSH ROYALFLUSH Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/20/2005&h=74a ....

First, I'll stand up and clap for the grid-making chops it took to pull off this incredibly difficult piece of construction.  And then I'll sit back down and confess that the challenge and joy that went into constructing it was not necessarily matched by the challenge and joy of solving it.  And that's because once I had stood up and applauded it, most of my work was done.

Still, the constructor has talent -- no doubt about it.

I want to focus on the one clue/answer that took me almost as long as the entire puzzle took.  I had "-EAT" for "position in a bridge game" -- and I so wanted that A to be an S, so that the answer could be WEST.  But AGATE was AGATE and that was that.  I ran the alphabet to finally get to SEAT and thought: "No, that can't be it!!!"  But, alas, it is.

Has the constructor -- or for that matter the NYT Puzzle Department -- ever read a bridge column?  Here's what you might get if SEAT is a position at the table:

"SEAT opened with one heart.  SEAT passed.  SEAT responded with two diamonds.  SEAT passed.   SEAT bid three no-trump.  SEAT doubled.  SEAT passed.  Question:  Should SEAT bid or should SEAT pass?"

Are you confused yet, Dear Reader?

Other than this (to me) quite ridiculous answer, this was an elegantly made puzzle -- especially so, considering its difficulty and limitations.  A nice job.

As mentioned for several days now, the app has been crashing on me every minute. That’s no exaggeration. I deleted and reinstalled but it did not help. It may be that it only happens during crosswords. Also, the link to Wordplay does not work.

I submitted a bug report and got a form email that was zero help. I’ve been a paying customer for over a dozen years. I would hope the NYT would give this more attention than a brush off. This is ridiculous.

Great puzzle! I didn't know what those circles were for until I had finished the whole puzzle, and then I saw what he had done with the entries at the top; wow!!! I am impressed. Very clever! Really enjoyed it.
I, too, updated the app yesterday and am continuing to have issues—crashed 8 times today during the Crossword (and once during the Midi).
Love being reminded of the existence of parallel universes and alternate realities, revealed through my morning crossword- and the surprise of breaking conventions- wonderful, thank you!
Such a clever theme!

There is only a small set of common interesting words that also spell common interesting words when spelled backwards. Like DESSERTS/STRESSED, DELIVER/REVILED, ANIMAL/LAMINA, and several others. I just learned that a term for these is "anadromes". More at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anadrome .

Crashing on iPhone and iPad, both updated to most recent iOS and NYT Games app. On both devices app constantly crashes at random. This was happening even before updating app/iOS and has continued after the updates. Crashes started when crossword was updated with new internal-to-app Wordplay "feature". Having Wordplay open in-app is ridiculous to start with, but the bug it has introduced has made my PAID subscription nearly unusable.

I updated the app on my IPad.  Won’t know if it fixed whatever bug was introduced until tomorrow’s solve.  

Oddly, the new menu page now has Wordle as the first game. Wordle is great, but it ain’t the Crossword!
Delightful and positivity enjoyable
App crashed, but that didn’t stop me from enjoying this delightful puzzle!

Hi Sam,
The ratites won't post my reply to your comment, so I'll try an original post. After the tech team gets the app fixed, could they help insure links to the Wordplay column are entered correctly each evening? Asking for a friend.

"71A. [That ship] is an odd phrase, but a neat bit of cluing to get us to SHE — assuming that “that ship” is the subject of the sentence, and not an object. Well, it is technically an object. It’s a boat. But I am repressing my smart-aleck tendencies."

I object!

Really cool. 

In some cases, the clues are more closely related than the author seems to indicate. Tesla, the car company is named for Tesla, the physicist. The term "pin" for a post is a direct reference to physical bulletin boards. And, of course, the two clues for Elan are just synonyms for each other. 

But that doesn't take away from the fun of this puzzle. Excellent Wednesday entry!
Fabulous puzzle. I am awed by the creativity. And, unlike some very creative puzzles, this one was a delight for the solver as well as a coup for the constructor.

Haven’t solved Wednesday, Tuesday or Monday yet but my only complaint with the revamp of order of games in the App is my Crossplay friends can’t find me and they really help me prep for Scrabble Club!
I couldn’t find Crossplay at first either.

Hey, did I miss something?

Have not read any gems from punster Mike. Any updates?

Hope he's just away on his hols.

 Ta very much.

A dazzling puzzle, complicated and fun to work.  I was prepared for a Wednesday puzzle, not too much and not too little, and it was a masterpiece of right down the middle.  When RAT was repeated at the top of the puzzle, I said the classic, "What the .....," then saw that the right side fills were the mirror image of left, and thought "Here we go!!"

Nit pick: I had a hard time taking out "suitably" for 68A, after I had given a hat tip to the constructor, and replacing it with MODESTLY, which was okay, but......

Jeffrey Martinovic, I hope we see your next puzzle very, very soon. Thank you for this one!
That was so fun!
one for the books breezy easy fun puzzle at the same time deep funny and brilliant thanks Jeffrey!

This one was a lot of fun! First I thought I must be wrong with my answers for 2D/13D. When I made my way down to the two thematic spanners, the "Aha!" moment was brilliant. Kudos to Jeffrey Martinovic!

Happy Canada Day from up north! 🍁

It was already annoying that I couldn’t rearrange the games so the ones I do are on top. Then you pushed the crossword down to promote the multiplayer thing I had no interest in.  Now you’ve pushed the actual NYTimes crossword down so far I have to scroll to it past whatever you’re pushing. That’s obnoxious. Please give paid subscribers control over their one app configuration!
Brilliant!

IMHO, which is a thing I never actually texted outside of this puzzle, this was a lovely puzzle!! I really liked the theme quite a bit. 

Makes me want to watch the episode of Star Trek TNG called Parallels again, season 7, episode 11. In short, anything that could happen has actually happened in a parallel universe, so there are hundreds of thousands of split off parallel universes where every single possibility is played out, and Worf ends up bouncing between them.  It's fascinating!  

Anyhow, my awakening to the theme also occurred with the second TESLA, and then the nickel dropped and I completed it speedily, and with great delight.

I think my favorite was the clue for SUSHIRICE. Cute one!!

It was crashing once each day. Now it crashes multiple times, every minute or two. It only happens with the crossword games. There’s no problem with the other NYTimes games. The same thing happens on iPhone and iPad, both using the latest software. It’s very frustrating.
Interesting puzzle and absolutely perfect photo!

poem made from words in today’s puzzle
a/ everyone really should care because it’s life. it’s just a little bit of life skating out to claim the life places where the milk glow of birth d/ might be people lying in a garden of clear sun might be the yawning spirit of a giant dog might be the used bed of the gentleman trickster whose a/ universe gets that alone and bored alternate reality aura… and modestly so
Since yesterday, the games app crashes repeatedly while playing the crossword. I have never had this problem before. Maybe roll back to whatever version was deployed a couple of days ago.
excellent puzzle!
I came here for the cat picture. Love it!

I solve on my laptop. Only tech issue is Wordplay showed up much later than usual.

Not a banner day, because I know less about sci-fi than I do about the soccer theme in today's bonus puzzle.

However, I may finally watch "Evita"--I found it online, saw the opening and read the synopsis on Wikipedia.

This was a truly staggering feat of construction. The two top corners alone would have been impressive, but sneaking in a WORMHOLE above both PARALLEL UNIVERSE and ALTERNATE REALITY? All while still having fun wordplay and clueing? Phenomenal.

This was an absolutely dazzling Wednesday for me. Thank you, Jeffrey!
Brilliant! Keep 'em coming.
It’s crashing repeatedly, and updating did not work
Loved this puzzle... very cool on so many levels :)
Very enjoyable. But can anyone explain clue 30? I can see LED but what's O LED?
Is the answer to 53 Down a stone?

Had the first crash today, but for me the crossword is still on top.

An apparently random selection of previous completed puzzles have been downgraded from gold star to blue, though, and my triple digit streak is down to nine days.

I long for a parallel universe where the CEO of the car company was one-tenth as intelligent and decent as the man whose name his company uses. I'm just glad it was ELAN in the grid and not that idiot's name. Nice puzzle, quick and easy.
Who is Liz Lemon? Don’t get the reference to Nerts. Never heard this word before  in the commentary it say Liz Lemon-esque
The cat as a potato - that put a huge smile on my face. Brilliant and cutest…
I love the sleepy Cat photo here! Definitely screengrabbed it for posterity such as it is.

I'm not sure Tesla counts as an "automotive giant". In terms of number of cars sold per year they are somewhere around #12-15. In terms of dollar value of cars sold per year they are also not in the top 10. Toyota sells more cars in 2 years in just the US than Tesla has sold worldwide in its entire existence.

They do have a best selling model, the Model Y, which is often in the top 2, but that's the only top seller they have whereas that companies in the top 10 in sales all have many models that sell well.
Very creative but a bit too easy for a Wednesday?
Both crosswords keep closing while I am using them. Seemed to happen after the latest IPad update. Very frustrating.
Well, that was just fun and extremely clever.
My problem is I feel that on NYT Crossplay somewhere they can’t find me.

I worked the cash register old-school style years ago. I’m fairly certain 48A was on the other side than what the clue indicates. 

Otherwise an enjoyable solve. I did not have problems with the app.

I loved how the top down entries all mirrored each other. That would be difficult to construct. I couldn’t recall whether or not you can have the same answer twice in one crossword but I see you can and more than once!
This is the first weekday puzzle with "zero flow" since Jan 1 2025, and just the second of the decade. Well done!
How the constructor was able to connect alternate reality and parallel universe is beyond me. this was such a cute puzzle! well done!
I truly adored this puzzle ❤️
Delightful puzzle. Great day.

That was really, satisfyingly good. The construction with 2 spanners, Wednesday level clues. I noticed the mirroring when the right corner started falling into place and immediately filled in the left. But then I had to stop, read the clues and see if what I thought was going on was correct and the jaw hits the floor.
nerts, nertz, gnerts? 1929-1979!
JEFFREY! That was amazingly clever what you did in the mirrored quadrants. Good job!
Really like this one, esp. the NW and NE trick.
What a fabulous puzzle!! I haven't really been on the wordplay blog since the change, but this puzzle was so fun and so clever that I just had to show my appreciation :-) more please!
Why did you move crosswords so far down the app?

I think this is my all time favorite puzzle, for the cleverness of the NE and NW corners ALONE.

One of the best. Symmetry of an unusual sort. Charming!
That was a lot of fun!
That was a lot of fun!

I don't know if I can find a WORMHOLE to a PARALLEL UNIVERSE, but I recently found out I'm a Canadian citizen by ancestry, and I'm working to get paperwork to certify it. So I might be lucky enough to live an ALTERNATE REALITY during the American election riots in 2028/29.

I loved this one. My wife had to help me finish a few tricky ones I had no clue about but I got the puzzle within the puzzle and enjoyed it. It reminded me of one of my favorite Thursdays from like 5 or 6 years ago, the Through the Looking Glass puzzle! That’s gotta be top 5 all time for me. This felt a lot like that one but with its own twist! Loved it! Best Wednesday by far for me!!
App crashes after updating.

Loved the puzzle. Somehow I was struggling with the NE corner after having finished the NW corner. Didn’t notice the trick until after it was done and wow do I feel dumb. 

Really cool.

What an excellent puzzle! I agree with many of the others; very fun to solve. 

Side note: the games app has now moved crossword down towards the bottom. Was this intentional? I get that other games might be more popular but I mean what are they without The Crossword.

@Dan
For no logical reason, when I got “Page Not Found”, I entered (for no logical reason) “Gameplay” in the search box and clicking on the first entry, which was Sam’s Wordplay column for today’s XW puzzle.”

Yes. When the normal link to Wordplay is not carefully arranged for solvers to find, I always go to NYT Gameplay (which is its own top search hit) and usually the Wordplay column will be near the top of that list.

As it was last night. But the previous night it wasn't even there.

@Michael Weiland

It continues to baffle me how often the NYT needs its crossword solvers to figure out what the url should be.

@Michael Weiland

Yesterday I gave up waiting for the links to be fixed. Today I did a Google search for NYT Wordplay and got here that way. I know budgets are stretched tight and there are more important things going on in the world, but is basic IT competence really too much to ask?

@Heidi

Apparently.  Is anyone else getting repeated Confirm Your Account screens when moving between NYT articles with a browser?

@Heidi

Someone at the times continues to think so. Why aren't the links tested?

@Heidi

Someone at the times continues to think so. Why aren't the links tested?

@Al  in Pittsburgh
Yes, and it's driving me crazy. I've had to sign in dozens of times a day.

I should not have been able to submit the same post more than once, but there you are.  And though two of the same posts were published, I got 3 email notifications for the same post.  

Saw several previous posts mention glitches.  Guess this is another one.

@Heidi In software development, particularly when maintaining mature code, there’s a saying: "All the easy stuff has been fixed." I can guarantee you that even when a bug makes it look like the code wasn’t tested at all, the reality will be much more complicated, usually requiring specific things to go wrong or happen in the wrong order, or the problem is a side effect of something that would appear to be completely unrelated.

None of this implies a collectively incompetent team.

@Ed H.

My suspicion, since we have had broken links from time to time as long as I can remember, is that the link is not generated by software, but data entered by a human, in which case, it should be tested or at least eyeballed by another human.

@Steve L
One of them could have been another automotive clue, but maybe that was too obscure for a Wednesday. Given TSAI, maybe not.

@Steve L

Sing a national anthem that doesn't require you to have a coloratura's range to reach the high notes. Enjoy some peace, order, and good government. And finally, un peu de bilingue. À la bonheur!

Felicitations, chère Petrol! I've very rarely succeeded at the acrosses-only trick and then only at kiddie-level, kiddie-sized puzzles. Enjoy and stay cool!

@Petrol

Here's my question:  If you wanted to stop approaching the solve unidirectionally, could you?

@Matt You mean, could i stop with my self-inflicted masochistic method? I guess so, yes. Any time i could just flip to the down clues. Perhaps some solvers like to wrap up a whole region of the grid before moving to the next. But i just have the habit of pressing on in one direction. I think on one particularly straightforward Monday i said to myself “wow, this one is falling into place so smoothly, perhaps i won’t even get to the Down clues!” And after that it just became a bit of a bonus challenge.

@Petrol

Any time you like, eh?

@Matt
Was that extra sploot of sarcasm because the cap came off the bottle or did you mean to pour it on that thick?

@SBK in TO Ha, i fear that i am too dense to read between the lines and understand what is being implied here. If the suggestion is that i could be “cheating”, well yes i could. But it would be rather pathetic to get my kicks from bragging anonymously about achieving something while knowing deep down that i didn’t achieve it. Who would i be kidding? If i get stuck and have to peek, i still enjoy the crossword, but i don’t claim to have done my “acrosses only” challenge if i didn’t do it.

@Matt Aha, I see! I normally give it a good go on a Monday or a Tuesday, but I have no expectation that it will work later in the week. However I do habitually start by going through all the across clues, so if a later day happened to look propitious, I might try my challenge. But on a Sunday, for example, I quite often jump between acrosses and downs, even on the first run through. So I guess my attachment to this practice is not yet a clinical concern! Phew!

@Petrol

Impressive.  I looked both ways and it took me 27:05.  I'll have to give the one-way solve a try to see if I can even do it at all.

@Jane Wheelaghan

@Jane Wheelaghan 
If I recall correctly, the pilot of the Enola Gay nicknamed the airplane after his mother, which might also seem odd, since he knew what its mission would be.

Surprisingly, the first ENOLA in the NYT Crossword was not clued to the name of the WWII aircraft:

Mon Mar 2, 1959	
52D	
Town near Harrisburg, Pa.	
Madeline Corse	
Farrar

(I know that ENOLA because the Pennsylvania Railroad had a huge freight yard there.)
Thanks. I didn’t know that. I’ve never heard of the name Enola apart from that.
Thanks! I thought Mo. was a city or state.
Thanks. Only older people I should think, experience a shiver at the name Enola.

@Paul Turner

His mother must have been a real blast.

@Jane Wheelaghan

Well, it is a state—Missouri. Just not here.

@Xword Junkie, 

714 straight years of puzzle solves is indeed impressive, although Methusaleh was telling me the other day that his streak stood at 832 years. My current streak is 8, but I'm getting there.

Xword Junkie,
You're the mathematician; please explain your math. If you filled the grid correctly in 15 minutes but it took another 7 minutes to fully understand the theme, isn't that 22 minutes total? And isn't the question -- as you posed yesterday too -- whether you should count 15 or 22 as you true solve time? Did I miss something in class that explains the 8 minutes? Or was that a Sam Loyd puzzle?

@Barry 

My puzzle, my arithmetic.

Just my way of having some fun at the expense of those who "solve'" puzzle according rules markedly more lenient than my own, and then claim to carry incredibly long "streaks".

(Still can't explain why so many folks apparently "cheat" at Wordle as well. Haven't done any statistical testing to confirm,  but the fraction of solvers each day who have solved the puzzle by the second turn (as reported by Wordle Bot' s statistical summaries) seems far too high to be explained in any other way.)

Embrace failure; it will help you grow (not @Barry in particular).

Xword Junkie,
Sam Loyd it is!
(I looked at the answer key.)

@Xword Junkie

Looks as if you and I are the only ones underwhelmed by the theme execution.  It just stops dead at the midway point of the grid, other than the redundant revealers.

I too spent a fair amount of time looking for more cleverness that apparently just wasn't there at all.

🤷‍♂️

@B

Yes.

PARALLELUNIVERSE along with the WORMHOLE worked very well to connect NW to NE.

But the second "revealer" added very little; I thought it referred to some, apparently non-existent, theme involving SW and SE. Oh well.

@Jeff Z

Well...it *is* because that's where the food is, after all.

But righteous point.

Could just be waiting for the server to come and take its order.
@Jeff Z Trying like heck not to become nigiri
We know that eels are lurking because they never post anything.

@Jeff Z

I have seen eels at the aquarium. They seek out twisty little holes and crevices and it sure looks like they are deliberately hiding there, waiting to leap out at unsuspecting prey. That constitutes lurking in my book. They are not hanging out in bright clothes in underwater street corners buying food in kiosks. I certainly don’t ascribe any moral reprehensibility to it, of course, that’s what Nature evolved them to do, but still, a lurk is a lurk.

@Jeff Z
It's, like, their actual behavior. Not an assumption. They (morays at least, which are your usual reef-eel) are sit-&-wait/ambush predators. Most of the time.

@SP
Kind of like our cat. Yes, he lives here. But when he sits in a corner of the hallway, staring at me from the next room… he is most definitely lurking.

@Joe It’s too much to ask for it 100% of the time. It’s not just making updates that work, it’s making updates that work whether a user has updated their app or not, whether they’ve updated their operating system or not, no matter which operating system or browser someone chooses to use, whether a user has added browser plugins that interact with how sites work, whether the code works with all variations of accessibility settings a user may have toggled, whether a standard code library they rely on changes something with little warning, and sometimes having to do this fast due to a newly discovered security vulnerability. Things look easy from the outside. Like constructing exquisite crossword puzzles.

@Steve L, Here! here!  And my two cents: Maybe (but just maybe), if we knew what the end state was going to be, or if we knew why these changes are necessary, or if we had a better sense that this isn’t self-justified tinkering by the design team, then it might be a tad more tolerable. 

But every other day these issues seem to get worse, not better. All the hard work from the editors at making the puzzles easier to get more subscribers is going to backfire when people start leaving in droves due to site instability (and worse).

@The X-Phile

That transformational, teleportational, metempsychotic Times crossword hidden-dimension-opening-portal experience is happening way less often than ever before in the last ten years.

This grid did try.

Which is something for a Wednesday.

@Rich in Atlanta
Wow, I don't know how I keep missing these, since I've done virtually every puzzle since about 1965. When I was in H.S. Lee Glickstein was my best friend's boyfriend, and he became my friend, too. Eventually, he moved to California, and we lost track. I ran into him a number of years ago at the ACPT, but I had no idea that he'd had a puzzle published.
@Times Rita He's had 26 puzzles published. Here's the link to his page on Xword Info: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Author/Lee_Glickstein ....
@Sam Corbin It was worse after I installed the update and re-started my iPad.

@Sam Corbin I updated the app yesterday and it’s crashing repeatedly today.   For what it’s worth, I’m not a fan of the rearrangement of the icons to place Wordle on top and the crosswords down farther.  I go back to the crossword occasionally. I never go back to Wordle.
@Sam Corbin continues to randomly crash despite the update
@John Sturm I had the opposite reaction: Finally Wordle is on top! (I always start w/ Wordle and have done every one in the archive)

@John Sturmi 
I agree!  The crossword is the cornerstone of the NYT Games suite, and as such, it deserves to continue to be placed on top!  Additionally, like you, I will usually return to crossword when I struggle with it at my first stab.  I tend to do Wordle all at once.

tpkmbw
@RozzieGrandma - "Organic light-emitting diode": https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OLED

@Pagrisan

Excellent question!  

A pearl is mostly calcium carbonate, a mixture of the minerals aragonite and calcite.  Calcium carbonate has those two different but stable forms or crystalline structure, and they are both present in pearls.

So, from that point of view, it's definitely a mineral, or, I commonly speaking, a stone.

And yet it contains a small percentage of a complex protein ("complex protein" usually means it's either to messy to describe easily, or we just don't know) which binds the purely inorganic materials together.

So, in that way, it's more like a biological structure.   

Nature has a way of mocking our attempts to categorize things.
